target: Two coupled fixes in n(r)/Gaia quantitative framing: (1) data/sections.json SEC-1.8 — wrong degree-conversion of 10 μas (off by 1000×); (2) data/wins.json WIN-016 detail_evidence — same Gaia-astrometry 'eight orders of magnitude' claim that ISS-780 already corrected to 'more than six orders' in sections.json, but never propagated to wins.json. Resolves attention-inbox ATT-2026-04-12T14:42:49.149Z (SEC-1.8 quantitative-framing-still-accurate verification).
patch_instructions: Two independent substring edits. Both before-strings verified to occur exactly once in their respective files. Patch 1 corrects an arithmetic error in a parenthetical degree-conversion (10 μas converted to milliarcsecond-equivalent degrees rather than microarcsecond-equivalent degrees, a factor of 1000). Patch 2 propagates the ISS-780 correction (eight → more than six orders of magnitude) from sections.json into the parallel WIN-016 prose, which inherited the original wrong number when written and was missed when ISS-780 was patched.

patches.before: The Gaia spacecraft measures stellar positions to ~10 microarcseconds (0.00000278°). Any medium with n significantly above 1.0003 produces angle-dependent apparent displacements many orders of magnitude larger than Gaia's precision.
patches.after: The Gaia spacecraft measures stellar positions to ~10 microarcseconds (≈2.78 × 10⁻⁹°, i.e. about a billionth of a degree). Any medium with n significantly above 1.0003 produces angle-dependent apparent displacements many orders of magnitude larger than Gaia's precision.
patches.rationale: Arithmetic check: 10 microarcseconds = 10 × 10⁻⁶ arcsec = 10⁻⁵ arcsec; 1 arcsec = 1/3600 ° ≈ 2.778 × 10⁻⁴ °; therefore 10 μas = 2.778 × 10⁻⁹ ° (i.e. 0.00000000278°, eight zeros after the decimal). The current text gives 0.00000278° = 2.78 × 10⁻⁶ °, which is the conversion for 10 milliarcseconds — off by exactly a factor of 1000. The corrected parenthetical preserves the pedagogical intent (giving readers a sense of how small Gaia's precision really is) while using the right number, and adds 'about a billionth of a degree' as plain-English anchor. The unit 'microarcseconds' is itself correct: Gaia DR3 typical bright-star astrometric precision is ~10–25 μas, so the headline figure stays.

patches.before: incompatible with Gaia DR3 microarcsecond astrometry by eight orders of magnitude.
patches.after: incompatible with Gaia DR3 microarcsecond astrometry by more than six orders of magnitude.
patches.rationale: ISS-780 (closed=fixed) corrected the same arithmetic claim in data/sections.json SEC-1.8 from 'eight orders of magnitude' to 'more than six orders' based on curmudgeon verification: at the sun's orbital radius the dome's n(r) gives n ≈ 1.235, so n−1 = 0.235; for Gaia consistency n−1 must be < ~10⁻⁷; 0.235 / 10⁻⁷ = 2.35 × 10⁶ ≈ 6.4 orders of magnitude. WIN-016 detail_evidence carries the same load-bearing sentence and was never patched, leaving the dashboard prose internally inconsistent with the kill-shot prose. Replacing with the same conservative 'more than six orders' phrasing matches ISS-780's resolution and the underlying arithmetic. WIN-069's separate 'eight orders of magnitude' (GNSS/baseline geodesy context) is arithmetically correct and explicitly NOT touched by this patch (verified: 'eight orders of magnitude' occurs twice in wins.json, only the WIN-016 occurrence is wrong).

holes_addressed.hole: ATT-2026-04-12T14:42:49.149Z asked the analyst to verify rest of n(r)/Gaia quantitative framing is still accurate after the ISS-780 patch. Verification surfaced two errors: (a) a parenthetical degree-conversion of Gaia precision off by a factor of 1000 in SEC-1.8, (b) an unpatched copy of the same 'eight orders of magnitude' claim ISS-780 corrected in sections.json, still living in WIN-016 detail_evidence.
holes_addressed.description: The decider's ISS-780 patch fixed the kill-shot prose in sections.json but the same claim in WIN-016 wins.json was never updated, so the dashboard WIN page and the part1 SEC-1.8 prose disagree quantitatively. Separately, the parenthetical degree conversion in SEC-1.8 carries an arithmetic error: 10 microarcseconds is converted as if it were 10 milliarcseconds.
holes_addressed.fix: Two surgical substring patches. (1) Replace the wrong degree value with the correct one and an explicit 'about a billionth of a degree' plain-English anchor. (2) Bring WIN-016 in line with the ISS-780 wording in sections.json.

kernel_of_truth: The qualitative argument in both prose locations is correct: a strongly refractive medium would produce stellar position shifts vastly above Gaia's measurement precision. The dome's n(r) at the sun's orbital radius gives n−1 ≈ 0.235; Gaia consistency requires n−1 < ~10⁻⁷; the ratio is genuinely ~2.35 million, which IS many orders of magnitude. Saying 'six' or 'eight' orders changes nothing about whether the dome's n(r) is ruled out by Gaia astrometry — it is, by a wide margin. Likewise, the parenthetical conversion was clearly intended to illustrate just how small a microarcsecond is in degrees; the intent was right, the number was off by 1000×. These are precision fixes to load-bearing prose, not changes to the argument's substance.

alternative_considered_rejected.alternative: Replace the parenthetical with no number, e.g. '~10 microarcseconds (a tiny fraction of a degree)'.
alternative_considered_rejected.reason_rejected: Loses the quantitative anchor. The whole point of the parenthetical is to give a non-specialist reader a feel for the smallness; substituting 'a tiny fraction' is less informative. Including the correct scientific-notation value plus a plain-English aside ('about a billionth of a degree') preserves both rigor and accessibility.

alternative_considered_rejected.alternative: Compute and assert a specific orders-of-magnitude figure for WIN-016 (e.g. '6.4 orders of magnitude') instead of 'more than six orders'.
alternative_considered_rejected.reason_rejected: ISS-780's resolution settled on the conservative 'more than six orders' phrasing precisely because the exact figure depends on which radial position one evaluates n(r) at and what assumed Gaia consistency threshold one uses (n−1 < 10⁻⁷ is itself an order-of-magnitude estimate). 'More than six orders' is robust to those choices and matches the in-prose phrasing in SEC-1.8 — keeping the two pages literally identical on this number is the point of this patch. We can revisit a tighter bound if a future EXP develops a fully derived Gaia-consistency threshold.

alternative_considered_rejected.alternative: Also touch the SEC-1.8 phrase 'angular displacements many orders of magnitude larger than Gaia's precision' to insert the same 'more than six' figure.
alternative_considered_rejected.reason_rejected: Out of scope. SEC-1.8 already uses 'many orders of magnitude' which is qualitatively correct and was implicitly endorsed by the ISS-780 resolution. Substituting a specific number into the SEC-1.8 prose body would re-open the very ambiguity the curmudgeon flagged in ISS-780. Keep WIN-016 aligned with SEC-1.8's chosen 'more than six' phrasing where it appears verbatim, but don't propagate a more specific figure into the rest of the section text.

anticipated_objections.objection: Why correct the parenthetical at all? It's a footnote-style aside; the headline 'microarcseconds' is right.
anticipated_objections.response: We hold the dome model to factual quantitative claims and bust them when they are off by orders of magnitude. Carrying our own factor-of-1000 arithmetic error in a parenthetical is exactly the kind of thing an adversarial reviewer of OUR work would flag, and rightly so. It is small in size but high in visibility (it sits next to the load-bearing 'microarcseconds' figure that anchors the whole section).

anticipated_objections.objection: Doesn't 'more than six orders of magnitude' weaken the argument compared to 'eight orders'?
anticipated_objections.response: Marginally, but the argument is robust to the change: a six-order-of-magnitude excess is still a catastrophic mismatch with observed astrometric precision. The dome model's n(r) at the sun's orbital radius is ~2 million times too refractive for Gaia consistency; whether we round that to six or seven or eight orders of magnitude does not affect the conclusion. Using the verified figure protects us from a curmudgeon counter-attack on the original 'eight' claim — which is exactly what ISS-780 was filed to address. WIN-016 is the only piece of prose still out of sync with that resolution.

anticipated_objections.objection: Could the original parenthetical have been intentionally giving milliarcsecond-scale precision (e.g., for context with older astrometric missions)?
anticipated_objections.response: Implausible. The sentence explicitly anchors on 'Gaia spacecraft measures stellar positions to ~10 microarcseconds' as the precision being converted. Hipparcos (the precursor mission) achieved ~1 milliarcsecond, not 10 mas. There is no plausible reading on which 0.00000278° is the right conversion of 10 μas; the number is simply wrong by a factor of 1000.

anticipated_objections.objection: Should we be checking whether other 'eight orders of magnitude' / similar quantitative claims exist elsewhere?
anticipated_objections.response: Yes — and we did. grep on data/wins.json finds two occurrences of 'eight orders of magnitude': the WIN-016 case (this patch) and a WIN-069 case in a different context (4,000 km baseline error / sub-mm VLBI geodesy), where the arithmetic does check out (~250 km positional error / ~mm precision = 10^8). That occurrence is correct and explicitly NOT touched by this patch. data/sections.json has multiple 'orders of magnitude' phrases reviewed: low-Q copper dome (five orders) and Eclipse duration are arithmetic-independent of this issue and out of scope.

out_of_scope_deliberately: ATT verification also touched on the '300–6,000× more refractive' phrasing. Verified: lower bound 300× corresponds to r ≈ 3,000 km in the dome's published n(r) formula (n=1.08, n−1=0.08, ratio = 0.08/0.000293 ≈ 273 ≈ 300×). Upper bound 6,000× corresponds to ice wall n=2.84 (ratio ≈ 6,280). Both bounds are accurate to two-sig-fig rounding. No patch needed.

EXP-096 (chromaticity bridge) was confirmed already-integrated in the sections.json text near the patched sentence ('Moreover, refraction is chromatic — different wavelengths are bent by different amounts. Gaia's optical astrometry agrees with VLBI radio astrometry to microarcsecond precision; if n(r) were real, optical and radio positions would diverge by arcseconds (see WIN-016)'). No further integration work required.

The 'n values of 1.2–2.8' phrasing in SEC-1.8 is a slight rounding of the dome's published 1.0–2.84 range (n=1.0 exactly at r=0 per the formula), but the qualitative argument is unaffected. Out of scope for this EXP; could be a future copy-edit cluster.
propagation_note: After integration the SEC-1.8 prose and WIN-016 detail_evidence will be consistent with the ISS-780 resolution. No other files reference these specific phrases (verified by grep on data/* and docs/* — docs/index.html is generated from sections.json and wins.json, so will pick up both patches at the next build).
test_dryrun_passed: apply-only-dryrun: substring replace + JSON.parse OK on both files

patch_apply_dryrun.result: Both patches applied cleanly to copies of source files. Patch 1 (sections.json): before-string matched exactly once, post-patch JSON.parse OK, before-string absent post-patch, new 'about a billionth of a degree' anchor present. Patch 2 (wins.json WIN-016): before-string matched exactly once, post-patch JSON.parse OK, WIN-069's separate 'eight orders of magnitude against mm-level GNSS' phrase verified untouched.
patch_apply_dryrun.test_run_skipped_reason: analyst does not run tests/run.sh per workflow division of responsibilities — decider applies patches and runs tests before commit. This EXP only verifies substring uniqueness and JSON-parse-validity post-patch.
